hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Reading a Bug Report -- Listening for Detail
lesson.menu_desc: Reading a real bug report and catching every detail in a developer's voice message -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 4 -- Reading a Bug Report
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 4 -- Reading a Bug Report

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: how serious a bug is, from minor to critical
the exact actions that make a bug appear again
what the software is supposed to do
what the software really does instead
to stop working suddenly and completely
the real, underlying reason for a problem
a temporary fix that avoids a problem until it is solved
happening only sometimes, not every time

paras: Yesterday afternoon, Ana was testing the new checkout page when she found a serious bug. She was adding three items to the cart, and the total was updating correctly. Then she clicked Pay now, and the whole page crashed. She tried again, and the same thing happened. The bug was not intermittent this time; it happened every single time.

Ana wrote a clear bug report. Under steps to reproduce, she listed every action: open the checkout, add three items, and click Pay now. She described the expected result, a payment screen, and the actual result, a blank screen. She set the severity to critical, because no customer could pay. She attached a screenshot and a copy of the error log, and she noted the environment: Chrome 120 on the staging server.

To describe a bug, we often use two past tenses together. Use the past continuous (was or were plus the -ing form) for the action in progress: I was testing the checkout. Use the past simple for the sudden event that interrupts it: when the page crashed. The longer background action takes the past continuous; the short, completed event takes the past simple.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, adapted from a real QA bug report

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The text explains how to design a brand-new checkout page.
Ana found a bug while testing and wrote a clear, detailed report; we use the past simple and past continuous to describe what happened.
The text is a list of Ana's promotions and career milestones.

items: Ana found the bug while she was testing the checkout page.
The text says: 'Ana was testing the new checkout page when she found a serious bug.'
The bug happened only sometimes, so it was intermittent.
The text says the bug was NOT intermittent this time; it happened every single time.
Ana set the severity to critical because no customer could pay.
The text says: 'She set the severity to critical, because no customer could pay.'
Ana forgot to attach any evidence to the report.
The text says she attached a screenshot and a copy of the error log.

parts: result was a payment screen, but the

gapfill.text: Remember: past continuous (was/were + -ing) for the action in progress; past simple for the sudden event that interrupts it. 'I was testing the checkout when it crashed.'

bank.label: Useful sentences to report a bug
items: I was testing the checkout when the page crashed.
The steps to reproduce are simple: add three items and click Pay now.
The expected result is a payment screen; the actual result is a blank page.
I set the severity to critical because no one could pay.
The bug is not intermittent; it happens every time.
I attached a screenshot and the error log.
The environment was Chrome 120 on the staging server.
We are still looking for the root cause.

lf.text: Use the past continuous (was/were + -ing) for the longer action in progress, and the past simple for the short, completed event that interrupts it.

items.situation: A developer asks: 'What were you doing when the app crashed?' Answer in one sentence.
tips: I was testing the checkout when it crashed.
I was adding items to the cart when the page crashed.

items.situation: Describe the bug's severity and why, in one sentence.
tips: The severity is critical because no customer can pay.
It is a critical bug; it blocks every payment.

items.situation: Give the two main steps to reproduce the bug.
tips: Add three items to the cart, then click Pay now.
Open the checkout, add items, and click Pay now.

items.situation: Say the expected result and the actual result.
tips: The expected result is a payment screen; the actual result is a blank page.
It should open the payment screen, but it shows a blank screen.

items: Report the bug in three sentences: what you were doing, what happened, and the severity. Use your notes.
Say it again and add the steps to reproduce and the environment: Chrome 120 on staging.
Now report the whole bug with no notes. Sound clear and calm.
The developer asks: 'Is it intermittent?' Answer honestly: 'No, it is not intermittent; it happens every time.'

answerkey.title: Reveal the model bug report
list: Model: 'I was testing the new checkout when the page crashed. Steps to reproduce: add three items and click Pay now. Expected result: the payment screen opens. Actual result: the page crashes and shows a blank screen. Severity: critical, because no customer can pay. Environment: Chrome 120 on staging. I attached a screenshot and the error log.'
Action in progress: past continuous (I was testing the checkout...).
Sudden event that interrupts it: past simple (...when the page crashed).
Report fields: steps to reproduce, expected vs actual result, severity, environment, evidence.
answerkey.note: Use the past continuous for the background action and the past simple for the event that interrupts it. Keep the report short, clear, and complete.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is Lucas from the dev team. Thanks for the bug report on the checkout. I read it this morning, and it was very clear. I have one question about the environment. You wrote Chrome 120 on staging, but I was testing on Firefox yesterday, and I could not reproduce the crash. So it might be a browser problem. Can you try one more time on Chrome and on Edge, and send me the error log again? And while you were testing, did you see any red error messages on the screen? That detail would really help me find the root cause. Thanks a lot.

Hi team, it is Sofia with a quick update on the checkout bug. Ana found it yesterday while she was testing the payment page. When she clicked Pay now, the whole page crashed. The severity is critical, because no customer can complete a payment right now. We do have a temporary workaround: users can pay from the old checkout page while we fix the new one. Lucas is looking for the root cause at the moment. The bug is not intermittent, so it is easy to reproduce, and that is actually good news. I will send a full report by the end of the day.

extra_audio.text: First, Ana was testing the new checkout page during the sprint. Then she added three items to the cart and clicked Pay now. Next, the whole page crashed and showed a blank screen. After that, Ana wrote a clear bug report with the steps to reproduce and a screenshot. Finally, she set the severity to critical and sent the report to the developer.
